payload.summary: Reviewed four amendments related to crash-resume durability in the engine's retry loop. Two amendments (fr-15-34731127, fr-15-bd667ff0) identify genuine bugs that undermine the correctness of the crash-resume mechanism. One (fr-15-1822a124) is a valid but low-priority consistency improvement. One (fr-15-ea35568a) is a useful diagnostic enhancement.

votes.rationale: Valid observation. After lines 4671-4672 restore active_run and set Running, the on-disk state remains Failed with interrupted_run set. A crash in the brief window before the next write_run_snapshot at the top of the loop iteration leaves stale on-disk state. Recovery still works (run resume picks up interrupted_run), so this is not a correctness bug, but the inconsistency window is real and could confuse external status polling or monitoring tools. Adding a write_run_snapshot after line 4672 is a cheap, low-risk fix that closes this observability gap.

Good diagnostic improvement. The classify_exit_failure function at process_backend.rs:1496-1501 maps all non-127 exits to TransportFailure, including signal kills where status.code() returns None. While keeping these retryable is correct per spec, signal kills from SIGSEGV (11) or SIGABRT (6) almost always indicate deterministic crashes (segfaults, assertion failures) rather than transient issues. Logging a warning when a process dies from a signal would help operators identify pathological retry loops without changing the retry semantics. Low risk, clear operational value.

Critical bug. Line 4643-4644 uses `let _ = run_snapshot_write.write_run_snapshot(...)` which silently discards the Result. The entire crash-resume path depends on this write succeeding — it persists the Failed status and interrupted_run data that run resume needs to recover. If the write fails (disk full, permissions, I/O error), the on-disk state remains Running with no interrupted_run, making the project unrecoverable by run resume (which rejects Running status at line 1155-1158). This write MUST be treated as fatal; propagating the error is the correct fix.

Real bug. derive_resume_state() at line 5852-5855 unconditionally creates the cursor with attempt=1, regardless of the interrupted run's actual attempt count. When a crash occurs during the backoff window (now survivable thanks to the new crash-resume code), run resume rebuilds the cursor with attempt=1, effectively resetting the retry budget. A stage that was on attempt 4 of 5 gets 5 fresh attempts after restart, potentially exceeding max_attempts indefinitely with repeated crashes. The fix should read the attempt count from snapshot.interrupted_run.stage_cursor.attempt (if the resume is for the same stage and cycle) and use attempt+1 as the starting attempt for the rebuilt cursor.
